FIRST_AID.TITLE: First Aid Guide
FIRST_AID.SUBTITLE: Quick reference for emergencies
FIRST_AID.DISCLAIMER: This guide is for informational purposes only. Always call emergency services in life-threatening situations.

CPR_ADULT.TITLE: CPR for Adults
CPR_ADULT.STEP_1: Check for responsiveness - tap shoulders and shout 'Are you OK?'
CPR_ADULT.STEP_1_WARN: If the scene is unsafe, do not approach
CPR_ADULT.STEP_2: Call emergency services (or have someone call) and get an AED if available
CPR_ADULT.STEP_3: Place the person on their back on a firm surface
CPR_ADULT.STEP_3_TIP: Move them only if necessary for safety
CPR_ADULT.STEP_4: Place heel of one hand on center of chest, other hand on top. Push hard and fast (2 inches deep, 100-120/min)
CPR_ADULT.STEP_5: After 30 compressions, tilt head back, lift chin, and give 2 rescue breaths
CPR_ADULT.STEP_6: Continue cycles of 30 compressions and 2 breaths until help arrives or person responds
CPR_ADULT.WARN_1: Do not stop CPR unless the person shows signs of life or help arrives
CPR_ADULT.WARN_2: If you're untrained, do hands-only CPR (compressions only)
CPR_ADULT.CALL_1: Call immediately if person is unresponsive and not breathing normally
CPR_ADULT.DONOT_1: Do not interrupt compressions for more than 10 seconds
CPR_ADULT.DONOT_2: Do not give up - brain damage begins after 4 minutes without oxygen

CHOKING_ADULT.TITLE: Choking (Adult)
CHOKING_ADULT.DESC: Help for a conscious adult who is choking
CHOKING_ADULT.STEP_1: Ask 'Are you choking?' - if they can't speak or cough, act immediately
CHOKING_ADULT.STEP_2: Stand behind the person and wrap your arms around their waist
CHOKING_ADULT.STEP_3: Make a fist with one hand and place it just above the navel
CHOKING_ADULT.STEP_4: Grasp your fist with the other hand and give quick, upward thrusts
CHOKING_ADULT.STEP_5: Repeat until the object is expelled or the person becomes unconscious
CHOKING_ADULT.WARN_1: If the person becomes unconscious, begin CPR
CHOKING_ADULT.CALL_1: Call if the object cannot be removed or person loses consciousness
CHOKING_ADULT.DONOT_1: Do not perform abdominal thrusts on pregnant women or obese individuals - use chest thrusts

CHOKING_INFANT.TITLE: Choking (Infant)
CHOKING_INFANT.DESC: Help for a choking baby under 1 year
CHOKING_INFANT.STEP_1: Confirm the baby is choking - look for inability to cry, cough, or breathe
CHOKING_INFANT.STEP_2: Hold the baby face-down along your forearm, supporting the head
CHOKING_INFANT.STEP_3: Give 5 back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand
CHOKING_INFANT.STEP_4: Turn baby face-up and give 5 chest thrusts using 2 fingers on the breastbone
CHOKING_INFANT.STEP_5: Repeat back blows and chest thrusts until object is expelled
CHOKING_INFANT.WARN_1: Do not use abdominal thrusts on infants
CHOKING_INFANT.WARN_2: If baby becomes unconscious, begin infant CPR
CHOKING_INFANT.CALL_1: Call immediately if baby becomes unconscious or turns blue
CHOKING_INFANT.DONOT_1: Do not blindly sweep the mouth - you may push the object deeper

BURNS_MINOR.TITLE: Minor Burns
BURNS_MINOR.DESC: First-degree burns and small second-degree burns
BURNS_MINOR.STEP_1: Cool the burn under cool (not cold) running water
BURNS_MINOR.STEP_2: Remove rings or tight items from the burned area before swelling
BURNS_MINOR.STEP_3: Apply aloe vera gel or moisturizing lotion once cooled
BURNS_MINOR.STEP_4: Cover with a sterile, non-stick bandage
BURNS_MINOR.STEP_5: Take over-the-counter pain medication if needed
BURNS_MINOR.WARN_1: Seek medical care if burn is larger than 3 inches or on face/joints
BURNS_MINOR.DONOT_1: Do not use ice, ice water, or very cold water
BURNS_MINOR.DONOT_2: Do not apply butter, oil, or toothpaste to burns

BURNS_SEVERE.TITLE: Severe Burns
BURNS_SEVERE.DESC: Third-degree burns or large second-degree burns
BURNS_SEVERE.STEP_2: Ensure the burning has stopped - remove from heat source safely
BURNS_SEVERE.STEP_3: Do not remove burned clothing stuck to the skin
BURNS_SEVERE.STEP_4: Cover the burn loosely with a clean, dry cloth or sterile bandage
BURNS_SEVERE.STEP_5: Keep the person warm and watch for signs of shock
BURNS_SEVERE.WARN_1: Severe burns can be life-threatening and require immediate professional care
BURNS_SEVERE.CALL_1: Call for any burn that is charred, white, or larger than 3 inches
BURNS_SEVERE.DONOT_1: Do not immerse large severe burns in water - risk of shock
BURNS_SEVERE.DONOT_2: Do not apply any ointments or creams
BURNS_SEVERE.DONOT_3: Do not peel away burned clothing

ALLERGIC_MILD.TITLE: Mild Allergic Reaction
ALLERGIC_MILD.DESC: Hives, itching, or minor swelling without breathing problems
ALLERGIC_MILD.STEP_1: Remove or move away from the allergen if known
ALLERGIC_MILD.STEP_2: Take an antihistamine (like diphenhydramine) as directed
ALLERGIC_MILD.STEP_3: Apply cool compress to itchy areas
ALLERGIC_MILD.STEP_4: Monitor closely for worsening symptoms for at least 1 hour
ALLERGIC_MILD.WARN_1: If symptoms worsen or breathing becomes difficult, treat as anaphylaxis

ANAPHYLAXIS.TITLE: Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergy)
ANAPHYLAXIS.DESC: Life-threatening allergic reaction with breathing difficulty or swelling
ANAPHYLAXIS.STEP_2: Use an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) if available
ANAPHYLAXIS.STEP_2_TIP: Inject into outer thigh, can be given through clothing
ANAPHYLAXIS.STEP_3: Help the person lie down with legs elevated (unless having trouble breathing)
ANAPHYLAXIS.STEP_4: Loosen tight clothing and cover with a blanket
ANAPHYLAXIS.STEP_5: Be prepared to perform CPR if the person stops breathing
ANAPHYLAXIS.WARN_1: Anaphylaxis can be fatal within minutes without treatment
ANAPHYLAXIS.WARN_2: A second reaction can occur hours later - hospital monitoring needed
ANAPHYLAXIS.CALL_1: Always call emergency services for anaphylaxis, even if epinephrine is given
ANAPHYLAXIS.DONOT_1: Do not give anything by mouth if person is having trouble breathing

FRACTURE_LIMB.TITLE: Limb Fracture
FRACTURE_LIMB.STEP_1: Keep the injured limb still - do not try to realign the bone
FRACTURE_LIMB.STEP_2: Apply ice wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling (20 min on, 20 min off)
FRACTURE_LIMB.STEP_3: Immobilize the limb using a splint if you must move the person
FRACTURE_LIMB.STEP_4: Elevate the injured limb if possible to reduce swelling
FRACTURE_LIMB.STEP_4_TIP: Use pillows or folded blankets for support
FRACTURE_LIMB.STEP_5: Seek medical attention - even closed fractures need professional care
FRACTURE_LIMB.WARN_1: If bone is protruding, cover with clean cloth but do not push it back in
FRACTURE_LIMB.CALL_1: Call if there is severe bleeding, numbness, or the limb looks deformed
FRACTURE_LIMB.DONOT_1: Do not move the person if spinal injury is suspected
FRACTURE_LIMB.DONOT_2: Do not try to straighten a bent limb

FRACTURE_SPINE.TITLE: Spinal Injury
FRACTURE_SPINE.STEP_2: Keep the person completely still - do not move them
FRACTURE_SPINE.STEP_3: Place heavy objects (like towels) on either side of head to prevent movement
FRACTURE_SPINE.STEP_4: If vomiting, carefully log-roll the person as a unit while supporting head
FRACTURE_SPINE.STEP_5: Monitor breathing and be prepared to perform CPR if needed
FRACTURE_SPINE.WARN_1: Any movement could cause permanent paralysis
FRACTURE_SPINE.WARN_2: Assume spinal injury after any major trauma, fall, or diving accident
FRACTURE_SPINE.CALL_1: Always call for suspected spinal injury - professional immobilization required
FRACTURE_SPINE.DONOT_1: Do not move the person unless absolutely necessary for safety
FRACTURE_SPINE.DONOT_2: Do not remove helmets unless necessary to access airway
FRACTURE_SPINE.DONOT_3: Do not twist or turn the neck or body

BLEEDING_MINOR.TITLE: Minor Cuts & Scrapes
BLEEDING_MINOR.DESC: Small wounds that don't require stitches
BLEEDING_MINOR.STEP_1: Wash your hands and put on gloves if available
BLEEDING_MINOR.STEP_2: Clean the wound gently with clean water
BLEEDING_MINOR.STEP_3: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth until bleeding stops
BLEEDING_MINOR.STEP_4: Apply antibiotic ointment and cover with a bandage
BLEEDING_MINOR.STEP_5: Watch for signs of infection over the following days
BLEEDING_MINOR.DONOT_1: Do not use hydrogen peroxide or iodine directly on wounds

BLEEDING_SEVERE.TITLE: Severe Bleeding
BLEEDING_SEVERE.DESC: Heavy blood loss from a wound
BLEEDING_SEVERE.STEP_2: Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound with a clean cloth
BLEEDING_SEVERE.STEP_3: If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top - do not remove original
BLEEDING_SEVERE.STEP_4: Elevate the injured area above heart level if possible
BLEEDING_SEVERE.STEP_5: Apply a tourniquet above the wound only as a last resort for limb injuries
BLEEDING_SEVERE.WARN_1: Severe blood loss can lead to shock and death within minutes
BLEEDING_SEVERE.CALL_1: Call if bleeding doesn't stop after 10 minutes of pressure, or if wound is deep
BLEEDING_SEVERE.DONOT_1: Do not remove the pressure cloth once applied
BLEEDING_SEVERE.DONOT_2: Do not try to clean a severely bleeding wound

HEART_ATTACK.TITLE: Heart Attack
HEART_ATTACK.DESC: Signs include chest pain, arm/jaw pain, shortness of breath, cold sweats
HEART_ATTACK.STEP_2: Have the person sit or lie down in a comfortable position
HEART_ATTACK.STEP_3: Give aspirin (325mg or 4 baby aspirin) if not allergic
HEART_ATTACK.STEP_3_TIP: Have them chew the aspirin, don't swallow whole
HEART_ATTACK.STEP_4: If prescribed, help them take their nitroglycerin
HEART_ATTACK.STEP_5: Be prepared to perform CPR if the person becomes unresponsive
HEART_ATTACK.WARN_1: Heart attack symptoms may be different in women - often less obvious
HEART_ATTACK.WARN_2: Time is critical - every minute matters for heart muscle survival
HEART_ATTACK.CALL_1: Call immediately for any suspected heart attack symptoms
HEART_ATTACK.DONOT_1: Do not let the person drive themselves to the hospital

STROKE.TITLE: Stroke
STROKE.DESC: Remember FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call
STROKE.STEP_1: Use FAST: Face drooping? Arm weakness? Speech slurred? Time to call!
STROKE.STEP_3: Note the time symptoms started - crucial for treatment
STROKE.STEP_4: Have the person lie down with head slightly elevated
STROKE.STEP_5: Monitor breathing and be prepared to perform CPR
STROKE.WARN_1: Stroke treatment is time-sensitive - brain damage increases every minute
STROKE.CALL_1: Call immediately if any FAST signs are present
STROKE.DONOT_1: Do not give food, water, or medication - swallowing may be impaired
STROKE.DONOT_2: Do not let the person go to sleep

SHOCK.TITLE: Shock
SHOCK.DESC: Signs include pale skin, rapid breathing, confusion, weakness
SHOCK.STEP_2: Lay the person flat and elevate legs about 12 inches (unless injury prevents)
SHOCK.STEP_3: Keep the person warm with blankets or coats
SHOCK.STEP_5: Monitor breathing and be ready to perform CPR
SHOCK.WARN_1: Shock is life-threatening and requires immediate emergency care
SHOCK.CALL_1: Call for any signs of shock
SHOCK.DONOT_1: Do not give anything to eat or drink
SHOCK.DONOT_2: Do not move the person if spinal injury is suspected

SEIZURES.TITLE: Seizures
SEIZURES.DESC: Help for someone having a seizure
SEIZURES.STEP_1: Clear the area of dangerous objects
SEIZURES.STEP_2: Cushion their head with something soft
SEIZURES.STEP_3: Time the seizure - most last less than 5 minutes
SEIZURES.STEP_4: After the seizure, turn them on their side (recovery position)
SEIZURES.STEP_5: Stay with them until fully recovered and alert
SEIZURES.WARN_1: Most seizures stop on their own - focus on keeping the person safe
SEIZURES.CALL_1: Call if seizure lasts more than 5 minutes
SEIZURES.CALL_2: Call if person doesn't regain consciousness or has another seizure
SEIZURES.DONOT_1: Do not hold the person down or restrain their movements
SEIZURES.DONOT_2: Do not put anything in their mouth
SEIZURES.DONOT_3: Do not try to stop the seizure

POISONING.TITLE: Poisoning
POISONING.STEP_1: Call Poison Control immediately (or emergency services)
POISONING.STEP_2: Try to identify what was swallowed, how much, and when
POISONING.STEP_3: Look for the container or product - have it ready for the call
POISONING.STEP_4: If the person vomits, collect a sample for the hospital
POISONING.STEP_5: If unconscious, place in recovery position and monitor breathing
POISONING.WARN_1: Some poisons are more dangerous when vomiting is induced
POISONING.CALL_1: Always call Poison Control or emergency services for poisoning
POISONING.DONOT_1: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by professionals
POISONING.DONOT_2: Do not give anything to eat or drink unless instructed
POISONING.DONOT_3: Do not try to neutralize the poison with another substance

HYPOTHERMIA.TITLE: Hypothermia
HYPOTHERMIA.DESC: Body temperature drops dangerously low from cold exposure
HYPOTHERMIA.STEP_1: Move the person to a warm, dry location
HYPOTHERMIA.STEP_2: Remove wet clothing and replace with dry layers
HYPOTHERMIA.STEP_3: Wrap in blankets, covering the head but leaving face exposed
HYPOTHERMIA.STEP_4: Give warm (not hot) drinks if the person is conscious and alert
HYPOTHERMIA.STEP_4_TIP: No alcohol or caffeine - they worsen heat loss
HYPOTHERMIA.STEP_5: Apply warm compresses to neck, armpits, and groin
HYPOTHERMIA.WARN_1: Severe hypothermia can cause the heart to stop
HYPOTHERMIA.CALL_1: Call if person is confused, has slurred speech, or loses consciousness
HYPOTHERMIA.DONOT_1: Do not rub or massage the limbs - can push cold blood to the heart
HYPOTHERMIA.DONOT_2: Do not use direct heat like hot water or heating pads

HEAT_STROKE.TITLE: Heat Stroke
HEAT_STROKE.STEP_2: Move person to a cool, shaded area
HEAT_STROKE.STEP_4: Cool rapidly with any means available - cold water, ice packs, wet towels
HEAT_STROKE.STEP_5: Focus cooling on head, neck, armpits, and groin
HEAT_STROKE.WARN_1: Heat stroke can cause brain damage or death without rapid cooling
HEAT_STROKE.WARN_2: Different from heat exhaustion - heat stroke is a medical emergency
HEAT_STROKE.CALL_1: Call for anyone with altered mental state after heat exposure
HEAT_STROKE.DONOT_1: Do not give fluids to someone who is confused or unconscious
HEAT_STROKE.DONOT_2: Do not use ice bath without medical supervision

BEE_STING.TITLE: Bee Sting
BEE_STING.DESC: Treatment for bee, wasp, or hornet stings
BEE_STING.STEP_1: Remove the stinger by scraping sideways with a flat edge (credit card)
BEE_STING.STEP_2: Wash the area with soap and water
BEE_STING.STEP_3: Apply cold pack wrapped in cloth for 10 minutes
BEE_STING.STEP_4: Take antihistamine and pain reliever if needed
BEE_STING.STEP_5: Monitor for signs of allergic reaction for at least 30 minutes
BEE_STING.WARN_1: Multiple stings or stings in mouth/throat require emergency care
BEE_STING.CALL_1: Call immediately if any signs of anaphylaxis appear
BEE_STING.DONOT_1: Do not squeeze the stinger - it may inject more venom

SNAKE_BITE.TITLE: Snake Bite
SNAKE_BITE.STEP_1: Move away from the snake to a safe distance
SNAKE_BITE.STEP_3: Keep the victim calm and still to slow venom spread
SNAKE_BITE.STEP_4: Remove jewelry and tight clothing before swelling starts
SNAKE_BITE.STEP_5: Keep the bitten area below heart level if possible
SNAKE_BITE.WARN_1: Treat all snake bites as potentially venomous
SNAKE_BITE.CALL_1: Always call emergency services for any snake bite
SNAKE_BITE.DONOT_1: Do not try to suck out the venom
SNAKE_BITE.DONOT_3: Do not cut the wound or apply ice

DROWNING.TITLE: Drowning
DROWNING.STEP_1: Get the person out of the water safely
DROWNING.STEP_1_WARN: Do not become a victim yourself - use a reaching or throwing aid if possible
DROWNING.STEP_3: Check for breathing - if not breathing, begin CPR
DROWNING.STEP_4: If the person is breathing, place in recovery position
DROWNING.STEP_5: Remove wet clothing and keep the person warm
DROWNING.WARN_1: Secondary drowning can occur hours later - always seek medical evaluation
DROWNING.CALL_1: Call for any drowning incident, even if the person seems fine
DROWNING.DONOT_1: Do not try to drain water from lungs - just begin CPR

HELPLINES.TITLE: Crisis Support
HELPLINES.SUBTITLE: You are not alone. Help is available 24/7.

SUICIDE_US.NAME: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
SUICIDE_US.DESC: Free, confidential support for people in distress

CRISIS_TEXT_US.NAME: Crisis Text Line
CRISIS_TEXT_US.DESC: Text-based crisis support for anxiety, depression, and more

SAMARITANS_UK.NAME: Samaritans
SAMARITANS_UK.DESC: Emotional support for anyone in distress

SOS_AMITIE_FR.NAME: SOS Amitié
SOS_AMITIE_FR.DESC: Listening and support for those in difficulty

CHILDLINE_UK.NAME: Childline
CHILDLINE_UK.DESC: Support for children and young people

INTERNATIONAL.NAME: Find A Helpline
INTERNATIONAL.DESC: International directory of crisis support services
